data.title: Finding Value In An Expensive Market
data.description:
Today’s topic is value, one of the quintessential pieces of investing. Benjamin Graham, a legendary investor who happened to mentor Buffett, is touted as the father of value investing. His philosophy of placing weight on the fundamentals of a business laid the groundwork for generations of investors looking to create and sustain wealth. Over the years, the meaning of value has changed and the term gets thrown around more often than it likely should. Nevertheless, value remains an irreplaceable piece of investing.

I think value has become more relevant than ever before. The past several years have reshaped the investible universe and today, things look fundamentally dislocated…at least from my perspective. With the S&P 500 reaching all time highs while other asset classes tread water, it’s time we talk about value.

Let’s Talk Value

Value, subject is derived from fundamentals such as current earnings, the potential for the growth of future earnings, tangible assets, and intangible assets. But most of these fundamentals are readily apparent and visible on financial statements, some like intangible assets can be much more difficult to value. Most assets, from a traditional lens, derive their value from the present value of future earnings. However other assets, such as fine art or precious metals, have no near term earnings streams and are thus praised based on the expectation that their value will continue to appreciate into the future. Understanding these fundamental concepts of value is an important piece of shaping your investment mindset.

On the other hand of value is price. While value is rational and derived from a thoughtful analysis, price is more turbulent. Price is instead driven by investor psychology and the ongoing battle between optimists and pessimists who hope to move the needle in one direction or the other. In the short term, changes in price are typically driven by investor psychology rather than a change in underlying fundamentals. Perhaps the perfect representation of this is the ongoing chaos with CLO equity funds like Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC). Despite being less rational, price is more concrete and the constant mark to market delivers the consensus opinion irrespective of anyone’s calculation of value. This leaves Price in a precarious position, often falling victim to irrationality.

While value has a gravitational effect on price, investor behavior is the ultimate day to day determinant. While the majority typically rules, there are periods when the contrarian has their day. It is my firm opinion that good investing comes from paying the right price relative to the value of an asset. Achieving superior returns requires one or more of several conditions including an underestimation of current value, pricing low relative to current value, faster than anticipated growth, and rising popularity irrespective of fundamental performance. I believe these are the fundamental drivers of uncovering value in a crowded marketplace, or in other words, delivering alpha.

Let's take this lesson of Value 101 and turn our attention back to the current market picture.

The Market in 2025

When we entered 2025, the S&P 500’s forward price earnings multiple was around 25x, above the historical norm and a level associated with lackluster forward returns. While I certainly wouldn’t categorize this PE ratio as bubble territory, I think most would agree that is a rich pricing level. The first quarter of the year was relatively quiet with little drama. Then things began to change.

Near the end of Q1, things began to take a turn for the worse. Pessimism began to grow in the face of slowing growth, stagnant inflation, and concerns that valuations could be too aggressive. The looming threat of a recession still remained as the economy continues to walk an everlasting tightrope. During a period of initial weakness, the S&P 500 modestly began to decline. Then came April, when President Trump announced an unexpectedly broad tariff policy, sparking fears of higher inflation and a materially negative impact on corporate earnings. It was at that point that the gloves came off and the market began taking serious damage. The market ultimately bottomed on April 8th, marking one of the most significant declines of the past 10 years.

From that point forward, the market has staged one of the most significant comebacks that I have witnessed. Since April 8th, the S&P 500 has staged a comeback of approximately 30% from lows, approximately 10% from the beginning of the year. Having been lucky enough to purchase during April lows, it's been an exuberant ride back to all time highs.

While the equity markets have held headlines for the roller coaster ride throughout 2025, other assets have similarly been on the move. As the S&P 500 fell significantly from the beginning of the year on the perceived future impacts of new tariff policy, the bond market was similarly impacted. Investors began to demand a premium associated with rising risk, and US corporate bond spreads spiked dramatically.

As investor fears began to quell, the bond market followed stocks and corporate bond spreads have since returned to tight levels. Despite persistently tight spreads, corporate bonds still offer an attractive opportunity on the back of rising interest rates. As spreads widened closer to the beginning of the year, the yield of the 10-year Treasury also increased by approximately 50 basis points. The 10-year Treasury spiked to a yield of 4.5% and has modestly declined to around 4.25%.

As described in the initial conversation and price, investor sentiment can often have a whipsaw effect, shifting like the wind. Just as quickly as immense fears around tariff policy stifling growth appeared in April, the market has become increasingly confident in recent months. Today, the S&P 500 has reached 30x, according to Multpl. Such significant improvements to investor sentiment were caused by the incremental rollback of tariff policy and accommodative tax policy which is expected to bolster corporate earnings, in addition to other headline growth drivers like AI.

The Price Value War

I believe fundamentals have deteriorated since January. For example, growth prospects are tempered, inflation lingers stubbornly, rate cuts are just a conversation, and fiscal spending remains irresponsible. Nevertheless, as the chart above illustrated, Mr. Market has once again charged ahead rewarding investors with all time highs. But what do we make of the warning signs?

Let’s talk about a couple. For starters, the earnings yield of the S&P 500 is around 3.3%, roughly 100 basis points lower than the risk free rate. Warren Buffett’s classic market cap to GDP ratio is also flashing warning signs at an all time high. The dividend yield of the S&P 500 is extremely low relative to the yield of treasuries. All the while, bond spreads have tightened to historic lows as investors appetite for risk continues to increase.

So, what causes these significant divergences between price and value? Why can the pricing of the market continue to improve while most of the available data suggests we should move in the other direction? More importantly, what do we do about it?

It is the inherently optimistic attitude of investors and a bias toward interpreting news positively that often causes investors to power forward. After all, as investors, we are here to make proactive decisions and allocate capital to companies that we think will succeed. We are looking for these opportunities, which also happen to open our minds to an array of biases that will affect our judgement.

With that in mind, I see the picture today as being somewhat worrisome given the exuberance of the S&P 500 against the macro picture. In fact, most areas of risk appear to be tightly priced offering little forward value. I even think dividend stocks look expensive. The question to me becomes, where can we find value today?

Where Can We Find Value Today?

There are two opportunities that I would like to present in the current marketplace. One has more risk than the other but likely offers greater return potential. However, both are what I would define as reasonably priced.

#1 United States 10-Year Bond (US10Y)

There may be no investment more humble than the ten year Treasury. Defined as the risk free rate of return, the ten year Treasury offers a benchmark for various assets across the spectrum. In fact, if you ask many classical investors, the ten year Treasury is the ultimate benchmark for any investment that derives its value from cash flow.

Over the past decade, the ten year Treasury has taken an extraordinary ride. If we were to look back even further, the persistent decline of the 10-year Treasury over a multi decade period has been one of the most significant contributors to wealth creation. Positively affecting nearly every asset class from residential housing to long-term bonds, the long-term decline of the risk free rate caused an appreciation of assets which acted as a rising tide to lift all ships. For generations, the ten year Treasury was an opportunity to place capital with no risk outside of interest rate volatility while collecting what was typically an above inflation rate of return. For generations, the idea of a risk free real return was a no brainer.

In the post financial crisis era, the Treasury began to look more precarious as the yield started to look tighter and tighter. Ultimately, the problem came to a head in the post pandemic era when the yield of these risk free assets had tightened to less than 100 basis points. Investors were staring down an era in which either returns would be nil, negative, or needed negative interest rates for any sort of valuation expansion. At that point, the ten year Treasury looked dangerous, if that's even possible.

Fast forward just a couple of years and the picture looks dramatically different. The yield of the ten year Treasury has expanded dramatically, erasing decades of decline. Today, the yield of the Treasury remains at 4.25%, higher than the long-term rate of inflation. Delivering a real return of approximately 200 basis points, the current ten year Treasury offers value in the face of an expensive market. From another perspective, the yield of the Treasury is higher than the current earnings yield of the S&P 500. This means the index implies significant future growth which is necessary to meet the return of a risk free asset.

From my perspective this is an obvious decision in favor of the ten year Treasury. However, there may be investors who invite additional risk into their lives in hopes of future returns.

#2 Realty Income (O)

O is the largest net lease REIT by property count owning over 15,000 assets around the world. The company has been actively expanding its global footprint diversifying assets across property types and geographies over the past decade. In my coverage of O, I often tout that the company has become a net lease index fund. Those who do not know O should refer to my last coverage below.

  • Realty Income: Time For A REIT Rotation

A common theme of my coverage over the past several years has been valuation changes due to rising interest rates. As I just illustrated with the ten year Treasury, the dramatic change in interest rates caused substantial repricing of assets.

Publicly traded real estate companies such as O saw some of the most significant impacts. For investors who purchased when the risk free rate was low, this means negative returns. However, for those viewing the company with a fresh set of eyes, it could be one of the best opportunities to dip into the sector in years.

Right now, O is trading at an adjusted funds from operation per share multiple of 13.6x or an AFFO yield of 7.4%. With an earnings yield more than 300 basis points higher, O has significant potential energy to deliver positive returns in a rate neutral environment. The company pays out around 75% of their adjusted funds from operations as a dividend, meaning shareholders get a monthly payment corresponding to a dividend yield of around 5.5%. Historically, this dividend has also grown at a rate approximately commensurate with inflation.

Comparing O against the broader index, we see a significant dislocation of earnings valuation. O is trading at a historically modest valuation while nearly every available indicator suggests the broader index is doing the opposite. Other participants also see value in long leased, cash flow producing assets. Starwood Property Trust (SWTD) recently acquired Fundamental Income in a $2.2 billion investment into the net lease sector. As capitalization rates appear to have leveled off alongside interest rates, the baseline rate of return is higher than recent history with a much stronger case to be made for future growth.

Conclusion

Fundamentals have begun to soften, valuations remain quite high, and psychology is as dynamic as ever before. I am a perpetual believer in the power of fundamentals. Right now, I look around the market and I see a battlefield where price and value are going head to head. Pricing across different asset classes is…inconsistent. Some assets are priced based on a Goldilocks scenario where things couldn’t possibly get any better. All the while, other assets like the ten year Treasury or O provide much more potential for future returns.

data.title: Astera Labs: A Premium Price For A Premier AI Enabler
data.description:
Introduction

Occasionally, a business comes along that is not merely riding a significant trend in technology, but actually takes us to a new level of it altogether.

In the middle of an AI gold rush when investors want to refill their pockets with the usual 'picks and shovels' plays, I think the wiser money is looking for the one business building the superhighways on which the entire enterprise depends. In my opinion, that business is Astera Labs (NASDAQ: ALAB).

And although the market recognized its success with a spectacular run-up to this point, I'm confident this story is just getting started. After a thorough examination of their technology and their financials, I believe the market has yet to grasp the sustainability of their growth and the durability of their competitive advantage. As such, this isn't just a great company, it is a fundamental piece of the AI revolution.

The stock is still a Buy with a path to $211.00 per share.

Company Background

To understand Astera Labs, you need to grasp the accessible key bottleneck that exists in modern-day computing. An AI server is not a single box; it is a complex machine of racks containing dozens of powerful components, mostly GPUs (graphics processing units) and CPUs (central processing units), that have to operate together in unison to be treated as a brain. The "superhighway" that connects all the brains is called PCIe (peripheral component interconnect express). You can liken it to a server's central nervous system, transporting volumes of data between its processors and memory.

So what's the issue? Physics. The faster you throw electrical signals down a copper wire on a circuit board, the quicker they degrade. After only a few inches of travel, the signal has become weak, distorted, and garbled like escaping radio stations preempted with undetectable static. In an AI server, where we want data speeds at the top of the rack CPU to the bottom of the rack GPU, this is a very significant roadblock. If the components cannot communicate with the utmost fidelity, the million-dollar-plus system is a huge failure.

Consider it like trying to have a coherent dialogue across a roiling factory floor. The communication starts off fine, but once it's safe on the other side — it's a hot mess. An Astera's retimer does exactly that, it acts as a perfect interpreter in the middle of a factory distorts the communication, accurately restores it to its original intent, then shouts it onward. In a multi million dollar AI server, you cannot have a single 'what did you say?' it has to be perfect every time. This is why I view Astera's tech not as having a 'component' in the big picture, but as having 'enabler' in the big picture. It is the lynchpin that makes the system viable.

This is where Astera Labs becomes essential. The company designs and sells a vital component called retimer. An Astera retimer is a specialized chip that acts as a signal interpreter/booster and detects the weak/garbled signal, applies the correct algorithms to clean it up and strip out the "noise," and retransmits a perfect/full-strength copy of the original signal to its place. The retimer is the link that allows the entire AI server to act as one. As management pointed out at its last earnings call, the integrity of the signal becomes more critical with every successive generation of faster CPUs/GPUs and more and more critical as projects leverage higher performance AI clusters; where their solution is not useful, it's essential.

Also, Astera is a leader in the next major evolution of data center architecture, Compute Express Link (CXL). As AI models continue to get larger, the next bottleneck is memory.

CXL is a new standard that allows CPUs and GPUs to efficiently use large memory pools as shared memory, breaking down the previous silos. Astera's CXL portfolio of products, Aries and Leo, provides the connective tissue for the new concept of memory sharing. By solving the core connectivity problems first for data (PCIe) and then memory (CXL), Astera now has the position of a foundational technology provider in the whole AI ecosystem.

Financials

In order to rationalize a premium valuation and a "Buy" rating, we must look beyond the story and examine the company's financial engine. This examination of the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow provides the base evidence for our thesis: it is a business of the highest quality and there is some juice left for the investors who wants to start a new position in the company?

The Income Statement

The growth story of the company is one of explosive growth coupled with intelligent scaling. The headline number from the Q2 earnings results is the $191.9 million in revenue, a 165% year-over-year increase. More importantly, it's accelerating growth, a tremendously positive signal that the company's products are at the adoption inflection point of the AI data center build-out.

Checking the income statement, the growth is generating profitability, which is more impressive. The company had a non-GAAP gross margin of 78%. This is an elite gross margin for any company, but particularly for a company selling hardware. This speaks to Astera's technological moat and pricing power. Customers are not buying a commodity; they are buying a mission-critical, enabling technology, and they are willing to pay a premium for it.

When I first saw the 78% non-GAAP gross margin in their report, I thought I had to read it again. For a company that sells physical hardware, that is nearly unheard of. This is something you expect from a pure software company. To me, this is the most obvious quantitative evidence for Astera's moat, indicating customers are not merely purchasing a chip, they are purchasing an assurance and they are paying a high, non-negotiable cost for it.

The real magic is occurring further down the P&L in the form of operating leverage. Revenue increased 165%, but non-GAAP operating expenses grew at a much slower pace. This is characteristic of a highly scalable business model. As revenue grows, the costs required to support revenue are increasing at a much slower pace, allowing profits to expand geometrically. This was exactly how Astera went from a non-GAAP operating loss in the previous year to a whopping non-GAAP operating profit of $75.2 million in Q2. This is more than just a company that is growing its sales, for me is a company growing into a highly profitable business and proving its model's long-term viability.

The Balance Sheet

In a notoriously cyclical semiconductor industry, a company with a strong balance sheet has not just an advantage, but the ability to survive and act strategically. In this regard, Astera's balance sheet is among the strongest I saw recently. Astera ended the quarter with over $1 billion in cash and short-term investments against no long-term debt.

In a world where corporate debt may be a hidden anchor, Astera is a speedboat. This financial fortitude provides Astera with more than just security, it provides swagger.

The absence of corporate debt is an independence I find incredibly attractive.

This has three profound implications for an investor:

Reduction of risk: The company is completely insulated from interest rate movements and credit market volatility. There are no interest payments required, so there is nothing to drain cash.

Strategic freedom: This substantial cash pile generated primarily by its IPO and increasingly through operations means Astera will be able to control its own destiny. Astera can fund its ambitious R&D roadmap for CXL and next-generation PCIe products for a number of years without needing to ask Wall Street for additional capital, limiting dilution of its shareholders.

Operational merit: As a result of diligence, working capital is disciplined. Receivables are increasing in line with a substantial sales ramp-up, not well ahead of the ramp-up, meaning that its large, high-quality customers are paying their bills. Inventory is lean, positively indicating products are going out at close to the speed of manufacturing, confirming the level of demand reported in that management meeting.

Cash Flow Statement

The cash flow has allowed Astera to really demonstrate it has quality in its published earnings.

The company generated positive cash flow from operations in the first half of the year. Quite a feat for a company doing something as extreme as hyper-growth! Why is this so important? Because it shows they are not just posting non-GAAP profits when the cash is actually flowing into their bank accounts to make it real.

When we take free cash flow and subtract capital expenditures (the cash it spent to maintain or grow its asset base) from operating cash flow, we see Astera generated free cash flow. For any growth company, this is akin to holy grail status. Said differently, Astera is sitting on a pile of cash, even after they paid all the costs and made all the investments necessary to run and grow the business. The fact that Astera can self-fund its future growth is what separates the good growth companies from the great ones, even if they are operating in traditional business models. Free cash flow validates the entire business model and provides in-depth fundamental support for the long-term projections we used in our DCF valuation. This ability to generate cash is why the market has assigned Astera such a high premium.

Valuation

A discounted cash flow model (DCF) is still the most conservative method for valuing a business like Astera, but reaching my $211 price target necessitates looking beyond a simple base-case scenario. It requires the belief that the AI build-out is indeed a paradigm shift, with growth runway and margin profile that will supersede all other tech cycles. It is a bet that Astera's current role as a foundational technology provider will become more important over time using the following assumptions:

Longer hyper-growth: My previous model underestimated Astera's growth potential. Since CXL is only just now beginning its adoption curve, I see it as a monumental degree of growth, not just an extrapolation of performance. My revenue growth model now sees 85% revenue growth in year one, 70% in year two, and 50% in year three, before tapering to a terminal growth rate of 3%. This model truly considers the dual-engine growth narrative of PCIe and CXL.

Elite profitability, software-like: As the Astera sales mix shifts faster toward more complicated, integrated CXL solutions, Astera's pricing power will only become better. I am adjusting my non-GAAP operating margin assumption from 38% mature-state operating margin, to a 42% mature-state operating margin. This should reflect Astera's software-like operating margins, because of its technological moat and mission critical nature.

De-risked by its dominance: while Astera is a start-up level company with zero debt, a percolating cash flow, and what borders on monopolistic level control of a critical technology, it is by definition lower risk than the average high growth stock. Its level of financial strength, profit level, and market position allows a bigger cushion for downside scenarios. Accordingly, I am lowering my discount rate (or WACC) to 10.0% from 10.5% to properly reflect this level of risk.

This triplet with a longer growth runway, elite longer-term profitability, and lower risk profile is aggressive, but I believe it is a reasonable consideration for a company that is itself becoming the standard for the implicit standard of connectivity for the entire AI ecosystem.

This is how these assumptions translate into a $211 valuation.

Our analysis begins with Astera's verified Last Twelve Months (LTM) revenue of $605 million. The model assumes year-over-year aggressive growth against this top-line figure and year one sees a revenue surge of 85% over the LTM, which brings the revenue to over $1.05 billion in year one, followed by a 70% growth figure in year two and a 50% growth amount in year three. To properly model a paradigm shift, we flow this modeling of performance for a total of a five-year explicit forecast period, assuming Astera can realize a run-rate of nearly $6.8 billion in annual revenue, as CXL technology will have become largely commoditized.

Next we estimate Astera's cash generation from the revenue forecasts. As the sales mix creeps toward more complex and integrated CXL solutions, patterning profitability towards the targeted 42% operating margin, this results in a nascent ramp in Unlevered Free Cash Flow (UFCF).

A company's valuation does not contemplate value using only the five-year forecast. We also must model a terminal value for the company to capture all of the cash flows from year six into perpetuity. Using the thesis of a 3.0% terminal growth rate with a 10.0% discount, which encapsulates Astera's de-risked market position, we can forecast future cash flows forever at an assumed growth rate.

The last step is to discount the future cash flows (both the first five-years of hyper-growth and the terminal period) and take the present value of these two forecasts.

Summing these discounted values produces a total Enterprise Value of approximately $34.05 billion. Then we simply adjust for the company's balance sheet and financial position, adding back the 1 billion in cash and subtracting zero debt. This produces a total Equity Value of $35.05 billion. Dividing by the 166 million diluted shares outstanding, produces our fair value estimate of $211.00 per share.

Peer Analysis

With a valuation of $170+ (06 Aug 2025), Astera Labs trades at the highest forward price/earnings multiples of the group it is being compared to such as Marvell Technology (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), etc.

Although Astera's forward multiples might look rich, the company appears to have a growth rate that is multiples higher than its comparables. The current stock price does not allow for any multiple expansion; any future returns from Astera Labs stock must come from meeting or exceeding the very high growth expectations!

Key Investment Risks

The main risk of holding stock that is priced for perfection is any indication that perfection may not be achieved. An insignificant miss on quarterly revenue, a small delay on a product ramp, or a general slowdown in cloud spending could result in severe de-rating of the stock's lofty multiple. Although it seems that the company is firing on all cylinders, the high valuation provides no cushion for even minor execution missteps. Customer concentration will also be a significant risk to monitor.

Final Takeaway

Astera Labs is not merely a participant in the AI revolution but it's one of the architects. The stock's remarkable performance is well-deserved, but my analysis indicates that the market is still coming to terms with the full potential of the opportunity in front of it.

My fundamental analysis, which utilizes a full discounted cash flow model ( including the company's two growth engines and solid financials) concludes with an estimated fair value of $211.00 which has some upside left from current prices.

The risk/reward profile is good for a new investment. Although the valuation is at a premium compared to peers, it is a warranted premium supported by Astera's astronomical growth and strategic advantage.

For the long-term investor, the conclusion is simple: Astera Labs is a Buy.

The strength of its recent earnings report is not merely a cyclical phenomenon, but an indication that the wider market is catching on to the story that the enlightened investor community has known for some time.

All that being said, the market is made up of different players and for the short term trader, the story is somewhat different. The technical posture for the stock is very strong. The great earnings report caused a powerful, 20% gap-up in the share price, representing strong institutional demand and momentum. The technical picture for the stock is such that the stock has a new and important support level, which is the low of the gap day at about $160.00.

This is setting up a potential swing trading opportunity. Although the stock is fundamentally fully priced for the long term, a pullback on declining volume over the next few days or weeks could offer attractive entry points for a shorter term trade. The thesis for such a trade is that the powerful momentum demonstrated with the earnings report will compel buyers to aggressively defend the $160.00 level of support. If the stock does test the $160.00 support and if it is successful in holding the level, it could then provide a powerful launching pad for the next leg up

data.title: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals: A High-Paced Launch That Could Grow Further
data.description:
The launch of Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:MDGL) Rezdiffra (resmetirom) for nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) has now passed through four full quarters. I rated MDGL a Buy in May, expecting a strong Q2 '25 for Rezdiffra, and noting the name was at the bottom end of its recent trading range. This article digests updates since that time and considers where the company stands now.

Another strong quarter

MDGL reported net sales of Rezdiffra of $212.8M in Q2'25, that's a $50.6M beat and a 55% increase in net sales from Q1'25 ($137.3M).

The company noted 23,000+ patients on Rezdiffra at June 30, 2025, up from the 17,000+ number noted in Q1 '25, and believes that they have 7% of their target U.S. market in F2/F3 NASH (or MASH, either term is used) on therapy. I can certainly see that the launch of competitors wouldn't necessarily steal current market share from MDGL, although there could be competition for patients being prescribed a new therapy.

Beyond growth in the U.S., MDGL has the potential to bring in revenues from the European Union. Notably, the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) gave a positive recommendation for Rezdiffra for NASH in June and the therapy could see approval from the European Commission this month.

It is possible we see MDGL bringing in $250M+ next quarter, which would already put it at a "blockbuster" run rate under two years into its launch. I don't expect the EU rollout (provided the drug is approved) would have the same pace to it, although I could be wrong. MDGL notes a launch in Germany in H2'25 as planned which would be a reasonable turnaround from a potential August 2025 approval. Beyond that, it'll be necessary to keep a close eye on the EU numbers as they start to roll in each quarter.

The peak potential in terms of addressable patients in the EU seems to exceed what is available in the U.S. For example MDGL has continually spoken about a target market of 315,000 patients in the U.S. with F2/F3 NASH, and believes that number would be 370,000 in the EU.

MDGL picks up an asset

On July 30, MDGL announced it had in-licensed an oral, small molecule, GLP-1R agonist called SYH2086 from CSPC Pharmaceutical Group. The drug is currently at the preclinical stage, with MDGL planned to get underway with clinical development in H1'26. SYH2086 is a derivative of Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) orforglipron. LLY recently announced results from the phase 3 ATTAIN-1 study of orforglipron vs placebo in over 3000 obese/overweight individuals with a weight-related problem (but not diabetes). Those results included up to 12.4% reduction in body weight after 72 weeks of treatment.

We don't know if SYH2086 will work better than orforglipron or worse, or if it will even be safe. An all-oral regime bringing weight loss and treatment of liver disease would be a great point of difference, although competition remains fierce in metabolic disease. Obviously the idea is to have a combination therapy that is wholly-owned, rather than partnering with another company.

The company is doing the right thing by building on Rezdiffra in my opinion. MDGL can't sit back and let other agents come onto the market in MASH without improving its own therapy for MASH (such as by developing combinations with resmetirom). Nonetheless, there are other agents out there with very impressive effects on fibrosis (FGF21 analogues, for example), and other weight loss drugs that outperform orforglipron (and so perhaps SYH2086), even if they have to be administered subcutaneously.

I don't know if MDGL has made the right choice to in-license its own GLP-1R agonist, rather than partnering with other existing drugs out there (already further into development or already in at least one indication). If MDGL begins clinical development of SYH2086 in H1'26, then we could see some date in H2'26, although the company hasn't provided a timeline yet. MDGL is set to pay CSPC $120M upfront for the drug, and CSPC is also eligible to receive up to $2B in milestones, plus royalties on sales SYH2086.

Additional financials

Despite reporting net revenues of $212.8M for Q2 '25, MDGL still reported a net loss of $42.3M. At the rate revenues are growing, one wonders if this could be the last quarter of net losses for the company. For Q2 '25, MDGL reported; Cost of sales of $9.1M, R&D expense of $54.1M, SG&A expense of $196.9M. MDGL finished Q2 '25 with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $797.0M, the company also had a further $5M in restricted cash ($802M total). The company did have $115M of outstanding principal from its Hercules loan at the end of Q2 '25, but repaid it on July 17 in full ($121.7M in total including fees and interest).

The repayment in full of the Hercules loan came as the company set up a credit facility with Blue Owl Capital Corporation. That facility sees MDGL draw $350M under an initial term loan, but also allows a delayed term loan up to $150M (available at MDGL's election until the end of 2027) and an uncommitted further $250M term loan (potentially up to $750M altogether). The loans mature on July 17, 2030.

Considering Q2 '25 cash of $802M, taking out the $121.7M used to repay the Hercules loan and adding in the $350M term loan sees pro forma cash of $1.03B for MDGL, with $350M in principal outstanding. Readers might also like to consider the upfront payment of $120M to CSPC Pharmaceutical Group to acquire the GLP-1 receptor agonist, although that deal is set to close in Q4 '25.

There were 22,289,014 shares of MDGL's common stock outstanding as of August 1, 2025, giving the company a market cap of $8.1B ($364.4 per share). The potentially dilutive securities as of June 30, 2025, should be considered, including; ~1.53M stock options (weighted-average exercise price of $113.86), ~757K of restricted stock units, ~104K of performance-based RSU's, ~2.4M preferred stock and ~3.6M pre-funded warrants. A fully diluted share count then might be closer to 30M shares when adding that to MDGL's June 30 share count of ~22.2M and MDGL would thus trade with a diluted market cap of ~$11B.

Looking forward if MDGL brought in $212.8M from a quarter where it ended up with 7% of its US target market on therapy, then it might be able to get $30.4M of sales out of every 1% of the market it gets onto therapy. If MDGL could capture 25% of the target market, that could represent $760M per quarter or $3.04B per year from the U.S. alone. This isn't considering the potential for revenues from the EU, or potential for revenues from F4c patients, only F2/F3 patients in the US.

A few notes on the above calculation, the numbers might be conservative since 7.3% represents the number on therapy at the end of Q2 '25 (23,000/315,000 patients) but 5.4% (17,000/315,000 patients) represents the number on therapy at the start of Q2'25, so a number somewhere in between, below the 7% figure I used, might have been more accurate. An estimate of 25% market share seems plausible given MDGL's calculations of a 315,000-patient target market come from patients that have been diagnosed. Further, MDGL has been able to capture 7% of that market in just five quarters.

Nonetheless, competition has to be noted, and of course while management believes the launch of GLP-1R agonists like semaglutide could increase awareness of MASH and diagnosis, it is possible that competition could offset that benefit. Further, other agents are out there which look competitive, such as FGF21 analogues. While GLP1-R agonists combined with Rezdiffra represent an obvious future combination therapy, which might allow MDGL to keep market share, FGF21 analogues and GLP1-R agonists also represent a potential combination therapy, the latter combination would mean no need for Rezdiffra in patients taking that.

Risks

There are multiple risks to a long position in MDGL, a few of which I'll mention here.

Firstly, MDGL could fail to impress with Q3'25 earnings since the company has set quite a high bar for itself by repeatedly beating revenue estimates.

Secondly, progress from competitors, such as trial readouts or regulatory updates, can trigger concerns that MDGL's market share won't be sustainable.

Thirdly, MDGL could engage in further business development activities, but there is no guarantee the market will be impressed.

Lastly, MDGL could report delays in EU approval of Rezdiffra, Rezdiffra might not be approved, or even if Rezdiffra is approved, there could be delays in the launch.

Conclusions and rating

I can see revenues in the billions in the U.S. alone, in F2/F3 MASH alone, as a possibility in the next couple of years for MDGL, despite the fact the company is only five quarters into its launch. I can also see $3B in revenue in the U.S. from F2/F3 MASH as a possibility, which I believe might only require about 25% market share, leaving room for competition. An $8B market cap (or even the fully diluted cap closer to $11B) doesn't seem unjustified then as there are potential EU revenues to be had.

Beyond that, the possibility of an expansion in to F4c MASH (compensated cirrhosis patients) provides further upside, where MDGL sees a target market of 245,000 patients in the U.S., not to mention F4c patients in the rest of the world, should MDGL be successful in its outcomes study ( MAESTRO-NASH OUTCOMES, data expected in 2027).

As for in-licensing an oral GLP-1R agonist, I don't know if this was the right choice, as opposed to partnering with an incretin that has already been developed much further, but time will tell. In the near term, in any case, the focus will continue to be on the launch of Rezdiffra in the U.S., potential EU approval and launch numbers and keeping on track for a readout in the. Of course competitor developments during that time need to be monitored. In the short-term, I think there is more room to run for MDGL, so I still rate the name a Buy.

data.title: ON24: A Value Trap As Customer Churn Continues To Decay Profitability
data.description:
Amid fresh new S&P 500 highs, it’s a time for investors to be incredibly careful about what stocks we invest in. On the one hand, large caps (particularly the tech megacaps that have dominated the market rally) are expensive, but where value exists in small/mid-cap stocks, we have to be very wary to screen for quality, especially amid a tougher macroeconomy.

ON24 (NYSE:ONTF), the webinar software company, is a stock that routinely fails that quality test. The company remains one of the few software companies to continually shed ARR, despite management’s repeated insistence that the business is stabilizing. Since the start of the year, shares of ON24 have lost ~20% of their value. In my view, this is a stock going precisely nowhere.

I last wrote a sell opinion on ON24 in May, when the stock was trading in the mid-$5s. Since then, the company has lost more of its core business, even as rivals like Zoom (ZM) have continued to find stronger footing on growth. With limited resources to meaningfully pivot its business, I’m reiterating my "Sell" rating here.

When we discuss ON24, one of the most prudent places to start is the fact that the company’s market cap is nearly equal to the cash balances on its books: In other words, the consensus in the market is that the company will keep bleeding resources, and the business itself is worth little more than book value.

At current share prices near $5, ON24 trades at a market cap of $218.3 million. Netting off the $179.5 million of cash (against zero debt) on ON24’s latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of just $38.8 million.

For the remainder of FY25, ON24 has tightened its guidance to $137.7-$138.7 million in revenue (-7% to -6% y/y), versus $136-$139 million (-8% to -6% y/y) previously.

This positions ON24’s valuation at just 0.3x EV/FY25 revenue, lower than virtually any other company in the software sector, and a clear indication of the market’s lack of trust in the stock.

Of course, I think that bargain valuation is more than justified since ON24 continues to destroy value and drain its resources. These are the core red flags to watch out for:

  • Relentless ARR declines. One of the fundamental benefits of a SaaS software company is the promise of recurring revenue. Yet ON24 can hardly even call its revenue base recurring, since its ARR has suffered sequential declines each quarter, reflecting the harsh reality that only roughly half of the company’s business sits in multi-year agreements.
  • ON24 is a tiny niche product that can’t compete with heavyweights. ON24's webinar software pales in popularity behind the much more popular Zoom. Especially amid tougher macroeconomic conditions, it's difficult to see why a company would greenlight ON24's webinar-focused software when the company is in questionable financial straits, and when rivals like Zoom offer more stability and multipurpose capabilities.
  • Dis-economies of scale. ON24’s slide in ARR has also been accompanied by sharp slides in adjusted EBITDA, which may soon slip over to losses in FCF, which more than justifies the stock’s bargain valuation.

Normally, software companies that fall to such bargain levels have a potential exit from being scooped up by private equity or a corporate acquirer. But in ON24’s case, its combination of decaying ARR and bleeding cash flow, plus a receding product, make it a very unappealing target, virtually eliminating any M&A potential for the company.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q2 download

ON24 recently delivered Q2 results that showcased a continuation of its unsolvable issues. Take a look at the Q2 earnings summary below:

ON24’s revenue declined -5% y/y to $35.3 million, though this slightly beat Wall Street’s expectations of $34.7 million (-7% y/y) by a two point margin.

The more worrying metric is the fact that ON24 continues to shed ARR. Again, consider the very definition of the term “annual recurring revenue.” It should be safe: A software company should generally be able to count on its customers to renew. And yet ON24 has continued to lose ARR for several quarters, with core ARR of $125.1 million for the quarter declining by $0.8 million sequentially relative to Q1 (where the company, in turn, had lost $1.4 million sequentially to Q4).

Still, management insists that gross retention is improving and that ON24 is on a path to stabilization. The company’s primary strategy to solve its declines is by chasing higher-value, upmarket customers. Without defining exact figures, the company notes that the count of customers generating $100k in ARR grew in Q2.

The company also notes that it's winning back “boomerang” customers who previously signed up for and then left ON24. Per CEO Sharat Sharan’s remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Our in-period gross retention was the highest in 4 years, supported by deeper customer engagement and expanded use cases. Our $100,000-plus ARR customer base grew in Q2, signaling traction in enterprise segments with high-value accounts leveraging our solutions to deliver pipeline and business results.

We saw continued win-back momentum from boomerang customers, many of which are returning and increasing their commitment with us compared to their previous engagement. Our product innovations, including AI-powered ACE solutions, continue to build momentum. A mid-teens percentage of our customers are paying for AI-powered ACE, a number that has grown consistently since we launched it last year.”

Still, we find it unconvincing that ON24 is a compelling product. Rival Zoom grew revenue at a 3% y/y pace in its most recent quarter, and Zoom was even held down by its worse-performing consumer segment. For enterprise, which is most directly comparable to ON24, Zoom’s revenue grew at a faster 6% y/y pace.

ON24’s revenue declines and ARR leakage have also contributed to a weakening of what used to be a profitable business. As shown below, adjusted EBITDA of $0.2 million declined -71% y/y, barely above breakeven margins.

Key takeaways

I’m a big advocate of rotating toward value-oriented small-cap stocks amid an expensive S&P 500, but ON24 is far from the right pick. This is a company that continues to lose business despite management’s insistence that it’s winning more upstream deals. This value trap is cheap for a reason: steer clear here.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Is It Time To Stop Quantitative Tightening
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has slowed down substantially its reduction in the size of its securities portfolio.

This curve, which shows the total amount of funds held in the Fed's securities portfolio, is bent. That is, given the slope of the curve, we can truthfully argue that the Federal Reserve is removing smaller and smaller amounts of money from its securities portfolio each week.

This past week, even the Fed saw the securities portfolio rise by about $0.3 trillion.

This brought the total amount of funds in the portfolio to $6,327.9 billion or $6.3 trillion.

The point is, the Federal Reserve has continued on with its efforts at "quantitative tightening," but the amount of funds leaving the portfolio...on average...every week is diminishing.

Now, we have to be careful about what the Fed is doing.

Early in the 2020s, the Fed put lots and lots of funds into its securities portfolio.

Here we see what happened in this earlier time.

This chart begins on January 1, 2020.

The amount of securities held outright in the Fed's portfolio totals $3,758.7 billion or $3.8 trillion.

The peak holdings came about around May 18, 2022. The peak totaled $8,504.2 billion or $8.5 trillion.

So, the Federal Reserve more than doubled its securities portfolio, bringing it up by $4.7 trillion at its peak in May of 2022.

The Federal Reserve began its effort to reduce the size of its securities portfolio, and it began a policy of "quantitative tightening" in March of 2022.

The Fed has continued its policy of quantitative tightening ever since.

From the peak, the Fed reduced securities funding by $2.2 trillion up to the current banking week.

So, beginning in January of 2020, the Fed pumped $4.7 trillion of funds into the banking system and has since taken out $2.2 trillion of money.

We are still $2.5 trillion ahead of where the banking system was in January of 2020.

The question is... has the Fed removed a sufficient amount of money from the securities portfolio so that it can proceed into the future with a sufficient amount of confidence that it will not set off the inflationary alarm again?

This, of course, is a highly uncertain situation.

The added concern the Fed now faces is that the Trump administration is setting up new tariffs all over the place.

Putting tariffs into place is inflationary.

As of the present time, these inflationary pressures have not shown themselves in a major way.

But...the bet is that the inflation pressures set off by the tariff fights will show up more strongly in the near future.

The presence of the tariff dealings has added a great deal of uncertainty to what is going on in the financial world.

The concern?

There seems to be lots and lots of money still around from the Fed's earlier move to pump lots and lots of funds into the financial markets to combat the threats unleashed by Covid-19.

But the Federal Reserve is slowing down its reduction of the securities portfolio.

Has the Fed removed enough of the money it pumped into the economy in 2020 through 2022?

Is it time for the Fed to consider a policy of "quantitative easing", a policy that would support the U.S. economy going into a period of higher growth?

The big fights with the Fed have, so far, all been about whether the Fed should lower its policy rate of interest.

The concern we are talking about in this post is whether or not the Fed should move a little bit more into the space of growing the securities portfolio rather than shrinking the securities portfolio.

This move would not be a move like the one suggested by President Trump. It would be a more modest move, flipping the current policy into one of growth.

The aim would not be to "flood" the financial markets so that interest rates could drop precipitously by two or three percentage points.

The aim would be to transition policy to one of modest expansion.

And, even here, we have the question about inflationary pressures and major inflation.

The tariff issue cannot be ignored.

Bottom line... Federal Reserve officials seem to be getting to the point where they believe the appropriate monetary policy is to allow the securities portfolio to rise at a very modest pace.

Again, how fast this increase would be is a difficult question to answer.

But we are getting near the spot where the Federal Reserve feels that it can flip from regularly reducing the size of the securities portfolio and begin to...modestly...allow the securities portfolio to begin to grow again.

If this were to happen, my guess is that Mr. Powell would not allow the Fed to regularly increase the size of the securities portfolio by very much at all.

Too much is at stake, and the threat of higher rates of inflation is very, very serious.

Anyhow, I think we are at...or near...that point when the Fed believes that it is time to allow the securities portfolio to begin growing again.

The effort of the Fed over the last three years or so has been truly a new era of monetary policy. For more than 40 months, the Fed has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio.

I don't know any other time in history when a central bank has carried on a "single" thrust of monetary policy. It has been truly remarkable.

The time to move on has always been a question mark for the policy. It appears as if the Fed believes that that time is very near.

But it may still be too early.

I believe that in this case, your guess is as good as mine.

The next year or so will be a very interesting time for monetary history.

Stay tuned.

data.title: EMXC: Emerging Opportunities Due To The Trade War But Significant Risks
data.description:
The ongoing tensions between the U.S. and China are redefining global supply chains, benefiting emerging markets.

In this environment, I believe the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ex China ETF (NASDAQ:EMXC) is a hold opportunity. The fund is focused on high growth markets and industries, and benefits from “friendshoring” trends.

I rate the fund a hold, because while I am very optimistic about specific areas covered by the fund, I think some others are not as interesting and dilutes the fund’s focus. My emerging strategy now is: an Indian ETF, South Korean ETF, and an emerging infrastructure ETF.

A Focus On Tech, But Diversified Industries

The portfolio of EMXC is highly diversified, tracking the performance of the MSCI EM (Emerging Markets) ex China Index with 659 companies and a 29.62% concentration on the top 10. Among the top 10, the biggest holding of the fund, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), has a significant weight, 15.24% of the fund, and half of the 31.6% allocation to Information Technology, the fund’s largest sector.

The emerging tech industry will benefit from the global growth in IT spending, projected to increase 7.9% in 2025 to reach $5.43 Tn. This will especially be the role of TSM through its dominant position in the chip manufacturing industry, leveraging A.I. and 5G adoption worldwide. The semiconductor industry is projected to grow at a 15.4% CAGR until 2032 to reach a size of $2.1 Tn, and TSM is poised for important growth.

The exposure to chips is further enhanced by the exposure to South Korea (15.2%) of the fund, which is a country where the market is driven by semiconductors and holdings like SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) acting in the memory chip industry, a key component for data centers.

The second important sector of the fund is the financial one (25.9% of the fund), the rest of the fund being a lot more diversified. Emerging markets are becoming increasingly digital, and this is also true for their financial industry leveraging digital innovations to ease the population's access to the banking system. The banking sector is also seen as being resilient with increasing loan quality and strong capital positions.

Nearly one-fourth of the fund is invested in India. I am optimistic about the Indian economy, that I covered here for another ETF. India is growing fast, estimated to excel in 18 high-growth, global and local sectors that could generate up to $2 Tn revenues by 2030.

As a peer, I will look at the Columbia EM Core ex-China ETF (XCEM), tracking the performance of the Beta Thematic Emerging Markets ex-China Index with 333 holdings. The fund has a 30.14% allocation in the top 10 driven by a 14.03% stake in TSM just like EMXC. The geographical and sectoral splits are also similar, with top geographies being India, Taiwan and South Korea. The fund is also focused on Tech and Financials.

Financial Metrics

EMXC is more expensive than XCEM (0.25% vs 0.16%) but I think it remains competitive given the global focus. The fund is very large, with $13.2 Bn AUM, more than 10x that of XCEM. This comes with a deep daily trading volume of $87.3 Mn offering very satisfying liquidity.

The fund distributes a 2.64% dividend, above its 30-day SEC yield of 2.02%, which is high given the speculative nature of emerging markets, and also high relative to historical yields:

XCEM distributes a similar dividend of 2.33% despite a 30-day SEC yield of 1.58%, in this situation, EMXC’s dividend appears to be more sustainable, in addition to being higher.

Looking at valuations, EMXC is slightly more expensive, with a P/E ratio of 16.18 above that of XCEM at 14.80. However, they have a P/B ratio that is almost the same: 2.16 and 2.15.

Now let’s look at the performance below:

Both funds have delivered important returns over the past few years. EMXC’s returns are good, consistently above 8% and even above 10% for the 3Y period. However, the fund’s returns are slightly below those of XCEM, despite having higher P/E multiples that would be rationalized by higher growth perspectives.

If the returns are favorable, they are also a bit disappointing. The rationale of investing in emerging markets is to get high risk high reward returns, here, it appears that the returns are resilient, but underperforming the S&P 500.

New Tailwinds

The tense macroeconomic environment introduces risks, but I believe that overall it will benefit emerging markets (except China) and this makes the fund’s positioning especially interesting.

I mentioned it in the introduction, the trade tensions between the U.S. and China are introducing “friendshoring” and rearranging trade dynamics around new countries. This means that the trade dynamics between emerging countries themselves, and between emerging countries and developed ones will increase, boosting exports, but also introducing a lot of volatility. However, to quote McKinsey: “Trade corridors between emerging economies could be among the safest bets”

The Indian economy, for me the strongest one among emerging economies, is benefiting from a strong government support for its digital infrastructure and energy sector, and should gain global market share thanks to the trade redirection. By 2030, we are talking about an additional $1 Tn, driven by manufacturing activities going from 16% of the GDP in 2023 to 25% by 2030.

Indian financial companies like HDFC Bank (HDB) and ICICI Bank (IBN) should also deliver outstanding growth, as the Indian GDP is growing at a high pace and as the country’s middle class is surging, both in terms of % of the population and in wealth.

Also interesting sources of growth, South Korea (15.20% of the fund) and Brazil (5.88%) are capturing a broader share of the friendshoring trend. Brazil’s economy is driven by commodities, with key holdings like Vale (VALE) benefiting from the iron ore demand that will be resilient over the coming years, with strong trends supporting its demand, but offset with a short term volatility coming from China that makes it an interesting opportunity to buy now as the market might not be pricing future growth correctly.

Additionally, the fund’s allocation in industrials and materials with companies such as Larsen & Toubro should benefit from the increasing infrastructure spending in emerging markets, seeing enormous investment needs.

Risks

EMXC is exposed to significant risks, and this is what prevents me from giving a buy rating.

Concentration is one of them, with Taiwan Semiconductor. I’m not saying it is a bad company, but the 15% weight is significant relative to the rest of the portfolio, and it also comes with a significant degree of geopolitical risk when considering the relations between China and Taiwan. Specifically, you can find this risk discussed here.

The fund is also concentrated on top industries, tech and financials.

For the tech part, a slowdown of the A.I. demand or a redirection of its interest towards developed countries could hurt the fund’s holdings. However, the diversification is offsetting that risk.

I mentioned the redirection of trade as a tailwind before, and if I believe this is the case, keep in mind that this is a disruption of global trade and that we don’t fully know if the overall effect will truly be positive. JP Morgan did an interesting piece about how this dynamic has conflicting effects, you can find it here.

As the fund’s returns are denominated in dollars but produced in many different currencies, this comes with currency risk and the fund’s returns will be dependent on the performance of the dollar index.

Conclusion

EMXC is an interesting fund, and a very complex one to understand. In this analysis, I gave you an overview of what I think is the most interesting about it, and there are really interesting areas covered by the fund.

However, I do not think the risk/reward profile of the fund is strong enough to justify a buy rating, far from it. If you are interested in emerging markets, I would recommend going for sector specific funds instead of a generalist one. This way you can build a portfolio with a more advantageous risk profile.

data.title: Parke Bancorp Offers An Interesting Combination Of Yield And Value
data.description:
Parke Bancorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:PKBK) is trading at a discounted valuation among regional banks and offers an above-average dividend yield, being therefore an interesting play across the U.S. regional banking space.

Business Overview

Parke Bancorp is a small regional bank, being the holding company for Parke Bank, which is a full-service commercial bank. It offers loan and deposits for business and individual customers in Philadelphia and Southern New Jersey. Its current market value is about $257 million, being a very small bank by this measure, and its shares trade on the NASDAQ stock exchange.

The bank is chartered by the New Jersey Department of Banking and its deposits are insured by the Deposit Insurance Fund, up to the legal limits established by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC). Parke offers several banking products and services to its customers, but it’s mainly focused on residential mortgage lending, commercial real estate lending, and construction loans. Its business model is quite simple, as the bank basically takes deposits and then lends to customers, with the vast majority of deposits being generated through its retail network and commercial relationships. Beyond deposits and lending products, the bank also offers debit cards, internet banking, and online bill payment.

At the end of 2024, its total assets amounted to $2.14 billion, of which some $1.87 billion was related to its loan book, representing about 87% of total assets. Its deposits amounted to some $1.63 billion, which means its loans-to-deposits (LtD) ratio was about 115%. As I’ve recently covered on Blue Ridge Bankshares (BRBS), the average LtD ratio in the regional banking sector is about 80% and, usually, a LtD ratio between 95-100% is considered acceptable. Given that Parke’s LtD ratio is clearly above average, this could be a warning sign that the bank is too leveraged, but that is not the case.

As Parke’s business model is quite straightforward, providing loans and taking deposits, most of its loans are funded directly through deposits while the rest comes from borrowings from The Office of Finance (about $150 million) and $43 million from subordinated debt. These are stable forms of funding and the bank is not therefore exposed to wholesale funding, being the most conservative way of managing its balance sheet.

Given this background, the bank is in a good position to grow its loan book ahead, if it decides to also grow its deposit base as the bank’s growth strategy has been mainly focused on organic growth, a profile that I expect to be maintained over the coming years. However, the bank has been quite conservative regarding business growth in the recent past and its geographical reach is quite limited, as the bank only operates seven branches, making its growth prospects somewhat muted. Indeed, its loan book increased by only 1% during 2024 and its total deposits were practically unchanged from the previous year, showing that Parke’s business is quite stable and the bank’s size is not expected to change much in the short to medium term.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, Parke Bancorp has a mixed track record given that its revenues and earnings have been somewhat volatile over the past few years. Despite that, its financial figures benefitted from higher interest rates over the past couple of years, a trend that changed during the last year as the bank’s average deposits costs increased at a higher rate than assets.

Indeed, its total assets increased over the past year to more than $2.1 billion, up by about 6% YoY, supported mainly by a higher loan book. Its total deposits increased by 5% YoY to more than $1.6 billion, which means its balance sheet mix has not changed much during the last year. However, despite its balance sheet mix being stable, the bank had to pay more for deposits, a trend that has been usual among smaller banks following the collapse of SVB Financial in 2023. Parke Bancorp has been no exception, and its deposits costs increased by a higher rate than its asset yields, leading to lower net interest income (NII) despite a higher loan book in 2024. Its net interest income amounted to $58.7 million in 2024, representing a decline of 9% YoY. Regarding non-interest income, it amounted to only $4.3 million in the year, representing a decline of 36% YoY, due to much lower service fees on deposit accounts.

Investors should note that about 93% of the bank’s revenues come from net interest income, making it quite exposed to interest rates, a profile that is not particularly bullish ahead considering that the market expect rate cuts over the coming months, a trend that is likely to have a negative effect on the bank’s NII in the near future.

While lower revenue is negative for the bank’s profitability, Parke Bank was able to report very good cost control, as total operating expenses amounted to less than $26 million in 2024, representing a decline of 26% YoY. The bank’s efficiency ratio was 41% in 2024, being among the most efficient banks in the U.S., a profile that is explained by the bank’s small size and relatively low number of staff, plus its efforts to develop online banking services.

Despite that, investors should be aware that Parke’s regulator has raised some issues about the bank’s anti-money laundering controls in the past, an issue that is not completely fixed, and this could mean higher costs in the future as the bank may need to hire more people in the compliance department to fix operational issues. Having said that, the bank’s efficiency ratio is much lower than compared to other regional banks, which usually have efficiency ratios in the 60-70% range, being a profile that is likely to be maintained ahead.

Regarding its asset quality, Parke Bancorp has a very good history with very low levels of credit losses, a profile that is justified by its large exposure to residential mortgages. Over the past couple of years, the bank has made net reversals of provisions for loan losses, showing that its credit quality is quite good and the bank doesn’t see any issues ahead. In 2024, its provisions amounted to less than $1 million, being a very low level compared to its loan book size.

Considering this background of lower revenues, but also declining costs, and a slight increase on provisions for loan losses in 2024 (from reversals in the previous year), its net income dropped to $27.5 million over the last year, representing a decline of 4% YoY.

During the first half of 2025, the bank has maintained a positive growth path with its loan book increasing to more than $1.9 billion, up by 4% compared to the end of 2024. This was key for higher NII in the period, even though its average deposit costs also increased compared to the same period of 2024. Indeed, Parke Bank’s NII amounted to nearly $35 million in the first half of 2025, up by 20% YoY. This increase was mainly related to higher asset yields that increased at a much higher rate than deposit costs, leading to margin expansion in the period.

Regarding costs, the bank has maintained a good cost control, with overall expenses increasing to $13.2 million in the first semester of 2025, up by 3% YoY. This resulted in an efficiency improvement, as revenue growth was much larger than cost growth, plus provisions for loan losses remained at relatively low levels. Its net income amounted to more than $16 million in H1 2025, up by 27% YoY, due to much higher revenues as the loan book increased and loans repriced at higher yields. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, was 10.3% during the first half of 2025 being a good level of profitability compared to other regional banks in the U.S.

Regarding its capital position, Parke Bank has a solid financial position considering that its leverage ratio was above 15% at the end of last June and its CET1 ratio was above 20%, both ratios being well above its capital requirements. This means the bank has an excess capital position and can return the vast majority of its earnings to shareholders on a recurring way.

Given this background, it’s not surprising to see that Parke Bank has a good dividend history, delivering a relatively stable dividend over the past few years, as shown in the next graph.

Its current quarterly dividend is $0.18 per share, or $0.72 annually per share, leading to a current dividend yield of about 3.3%. While this is not a high-dividend yield, it’s above-average compared to the banking sector and seems to be sustainable over the long term, as the bank is very well capitalized and its dividend payout ratio is below 30%, being a very conservative level. This means the bank could easily increase its dividend ahead, even though its strategy has been to maintain its dividend unchanged over the past few years, despite being more than able to distribute larger dividends to shareholders.

Regarding its valuation, Parke Bank seems to be currently undervalued as its shares are trading at only 8x earnings and 0.82x book value. Both metrics are at a significant discount to peers, which doesn’t seem to be justified given that the bank has a defensive business profile and offers an interesting dividend yield.

Conclusion

Parke Bank is a small regional bank that is highly exposed to low-risk residential mortgages, leading to a very simple business profile and very low levels of credit losses. While its growth prospects are also quite muted as the bank is not likely to change much its geographical exposure over the next few years, Parke Bank offers a sustainable dividend and an above-average yield, plus its valuation seems to be cheap, making it an interesting play among small regional banks in the U.S.

data.title: Real Retail Sales Continue To Run
data.description:
Real Retail Sales are holding steady but on an uptrend from April 2024. The past 2mos saw higher revisions. There remains no support for the oft-repeated “recession around the corner” many perceive using manufacturing PMI below 50 since Nov. 2022 and currently sitting at 48. There was a noted boost in Light Weight Vehicle Sales & Parts with the 16.411Mil SAAR (Seasonally Adjusted Annual Rate) coming in 1mil above the forecasted 15.3Mil level. Cooper-Standard (CPS) remains significantly underpriced should sales exceed forecasts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KULR Technology: Shareholder Dilution Continues Unabatedly - Sell
data.description:
KULR Technology Overview

Note:

I have covered KULR Technology Group, Inc. (NYSE:KULR) or “KULR Technology” previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

After the close of Thursday's regular session, KULR Technology reported second quarter results well above consensus expectations:

Revenues of $4.0 million represented a new all-time high for the company while GAAP earnings per share of $0.22 also coming in well above the ($0.12) consensus estimate.

However, adjusted for contributions related to KULR Technology's recently initiated Bitcoin treasury strategy, revenues were up just slightly on a year-over-year basis while the company's loss from operations increased by almost 80% to $9.5 million.

Record cash burn of $12.1 million wasn't suited to instill confidence in the core business, either.

During the quarter, KULR Technology raised an additional $38.3 million from selling approximately 3.8 million newly-issued shares into the open market. Subsequent to quarter-end, the company sold an additional 1.6 million shares into the open market for gross proceeds of $10.7 million.

Please note that KULR Technology conducted an 1:8 reverse stock split on June 20.

Most of the proceeds were used to purchase additional Bitcoin. As of the end of the second quarter, the company held 928.28 Bitcoin with a market value of $99.5 million. Subsequent to quarter-end, KULR Technology increased its total Bitcoin holdings to 1,035 with an aggregate market value of $121.3 million.

The company has also entered into a number of Bitcoin mining services agreements, as outlined in more detail in its quarterly report on form 10-Q:

On March 7, 2025, the Company entered into a sixty-day lease agreement (the “First Machine Lease Agreement”) with a digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $850,000.

Additionally, on May 16, 2025, the Company entered into a two hundred and twenty eight-day lease agreement (the “Second Machine Lease Agreement”) with the same digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $3,200,000.

Furthermore, on June 20, 2025, the Company entered into a one hundred and three-day lease agreement (the “Third Machine Lease Agreement”) with a new digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $2,756,795. During the three and six months ended June 30, 2025, the Company recognized revenue of $1,439,095 and $1,688,849, respectively, in connection with its digital assets mining operations.

While only a limited number of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) will be mined under these agreements, they enable the company to recognize mining revenues. Due to the additional lease agreements signed in the second quarter, I would expect Bitcoin mining revenues to increase to approximately $3.6 million in the third quarter thus covering more than 70% of the current consensus estimate.

During the questions-and-answers session of the conference call, a number of investors complained hard about the persistent lack of operational progress, broken promises, aggressive dilution and the disastrous share price performance. Quite frankly, I can't blame them:

Following the most recent stock price decline, KULR Technology's valuation has declined to more reasonable levels:

However given the lack of operational progress and substantially negative free cash flow, I am struggling to assign any sort of meaningful value to the tiny core business at this point.

In addition, the company just entered into a massive $300 million open market sales agreement with Cantor Fitzgerald & Co. and Craig-Hallum Capital Group, LLC. As a result, I would expect shareholder dilution to continue unabatedly for the time being.

At this point, KULR Technology remains almost solely a bet on the Bitcoin price appreciating even further. But even with Bitcoin trading near all-time highs, the company's stock price has continued to decline due to persistent open market sales which are not likely to stop anytime soon.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on KULR Technology's common shares.

Risk Factors

As usual, the bear thesis is not without risk. The most obvious catalysts for the stock are:

  • termination of the company's open market sales agreement.
  • further Bitcoin price appreciation.
  • substantial improvements in the company's operating performance.

Particularly the removal of the open market sales agreement overhang would likely attract a new round of speculative investors and traders to the stock.

Bottom Line

KULR Technology reported second quarter earnings well ahead of expectations. However, record revenues and profitability were solely the result of the company's recently initiated Bitcoin treasury strategy. In fact, operating results showed record losses and persistent cash burn.

Given the lack of progress in the core business and ongoing overhang from aggressive open market sales, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on KULR Technology's common shares.

data.title: IVES: Too Big Tech Focused
data.description:
For investors unfamiliar with Dan Ives, he is always easy to spot on CNBC or other media outlets as he’s often wearing outlandish and eye-catching clothing. Despite Ives' questionable attire selection he is one of the most prominent tech analysts today with more than two decades worth of experience researching the tech sector. Ives has been correct on numerous bullish tech calls including, Palantir (PLTR) and Tesla (TSLA)

Earlier this summer, Ives and the team at Wedbush launched the Dan Ives Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (NYSEARCA:IVES). Let’s dive into the fund’s strategy and current top holdings as I discuss why this ETF may be well-suited for some growth investors, especially those looking to get some AI exposure.

Fund Strategy and Holdings

The ETF’s strategy is simple, find the companies that are poised to lead the artificial intelligence revolution. Ives believes tech investing is undergoing a “golden age” with the emergence of AI and that will generate huge returns for investors.

When discussing his ETF Ives stated, “I believe the market is still massively underestimating what the growth is going to look like for the AI revolution in tech. For us, it’s not just Mag 7. It’s not just those first four or five names. It’s trying to identify names that maybe today thematically you don’t even consider an AI name.”

The fund currently has 30 stocks including many Mag 7 and chip stocks known to tech investors:

Looking at the top holdings in IVES, I can quickly see that this fund is heavy in the Mag 7 and I’d argue the first twelve holdings are large, well-known companies. Looking down the list further, you’ll see more familiar names such as Salesforce (CRM), IBM, and Adobe (ADBE). To me, the theme of this ETF is that big tech will continue to lead the AI boom as these firms have the capital to continue to spend.

This chart from the Wall Street Journal also clearly illustrates tech giants, Meta, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon are splurging on AI capital expenditures:

Many investors have likely seen the news of Meta trying to poach AI talent by offering them ridiculous salaries, all in the hopes of winning the big tech AI arms race. Clearly, big tech is spending, spending, spending, in hopes of dominating the AI revolution.

Now, there are some issues I have with the fund being so heavy into large, established tech giants.

First, let's discuss if this spending will result in AI dominance. In a blog post, popular finance writer, Ben Carlson noted two probable outcomes associated with AI spending. One outcome is that these tech giants dominate because obviously they spent so much money on this world-changing technology. The second outcome is that these companies crash and burn because the ROI was less than predicted and expectations were far too high. Obviously, it doesn't have to be one or the other either, but I think Carlson does bring up a good point that spending doesn't ensure future success. One need look no further than Zuckerberg's metaverse spending.

Next, I think it’s a miss this fund isn’t including many stocks associated with the infrastructure necessary for these tech giants to continue to power their data centers and their overall AI efforts. The ETF does include Oklo, Inc. (OKLO) which I’d say is still a speculation stock. Whereas, Tom Lee’s Granny Shot ETF (GRNY) has more solid energy and infrastructure plays such as GE Vernova (GEV), Quanta Services (PWR), Eaton (ETN) and Emerson Electric (EMR).
